figures.caption: Figure 1: Sketch of the different use of data during training, including dataset expansion with advection hypothesis, and unsupervised training for unlabelled snapshots.
Table 1: The model structure used for the channel dataset

Figure 2: Layout of the channel flow dataset. The dyed blocks indicate subdomains, and the red crosses the positions for synthetic probes. The domain is not represented in the full streamwise length of 8πh for ease of representation.

Figure 3: Left: the POD squared singular values (left), with the descending curve showing the energy contained in each POD mode and ascending curve showing the cumulative energy. Right: Reconstruction error from LOR in the testing dataset. The horizontal axis is displayed in logarithmic scale showing the mode numbers.

Table 2: Hyperparameters used in the channel case. LP: propagation length (number of propagated frames). CP: ratio between propagated and supervised datasets. CU: ratio of (supervised + propagated) to unsupervised data.

Figure 4: The ground truth and predicted ψ of mode 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 32, 128, 512 (from 1st to 2nd row), normalized by 1/ √ nt. The ground truth and predicted ψt of mode 1, 2, 3, 4, 8, 32, 128 (from 3rd to 4th row), normalized by 1/ √ nt. The black horizontal line locates the position of 0.

Figure 5: The cosine similarity of each predicted POD temporal mode ψ to the ground truth (left), the logarithmic ratio of RMS of predicted POD temporal mode ψ to the counter part of ground truth (right). The horizontal black line showing the position of 0, while the horizontal axis is rescaled by the singular value Σ. Data from the simulation dataset of turbulent channel flow.

Figure 6: The ground truth (the 1st column) and predicted (from the 2nd column) flow field from the channel data set, from top to bottom, the three components of the velocity field and pressure field. All fields are normalized, and are displayed with a vertical slice and an horizontal slice. The crossing signs on the up-left subplot stands for the position of probes.
Figure 7: Turbulent channel flow dataset. Temporal evolution of reconstruction errors for the velocity field (left) and pressure field (right).

Figure 8: PDFs of fluctuating streamwise velocity and pressure for ground truth, LOR, and all reconstructions, normalized by RMS of u′ GT or p′ GT and evaluated over all testing frames of the turbulent channel flow dataset.

Figure 9: Cosine similarity (columns 1, 3) and logarithmic RMS fluctuation ratio (columns 2, 4) of the temporally averaged streamwise velocity (columns 1–2) and pressure (columns 3–4) fields. Top: LOR versus ground truth. Bottom: semi-supervised ML, with regularization, reconstruction trained on unlabelled samples versus LOR.
Figure 10: Power spectra of the velocity field for GT, LOR, and all reconstruction techniques. Top: temporal frequency spectra; bottom: wavenumber spectra. Columns show the three velocity components.
Table 4: The model structure of the airfoil wake dataset
Table 5: Hyperparameters used in the wing case.
Figure 11: POD eigenspectrum and cumulative energy versus mode number (left) for the wing wake dataset. Relative RMS error of LOR (right).

Figure 12: Comparison of PIV reference and predicted spatial modes ψ (top two rows) and temporal modes ψt (bottom two rows) for selected mode numbers for the wing wake dataset. All modes are normalized by 1/ √ nt, and the black horizontal line marks zero.

Figure 13: Cosine similarity of predicted POD temporal modes ψ with respect to the PIV field (left) and their logarithmic RMS ratio relative to the PIV field (right) for the wing wake dataset. The horizontal black line denotes zero, and the horizontal axis is rescaled by the singular value Σ.

Figure 14: Comparison of ground-truth (first column) and predicted (remaining columns) normalized flow fields for the wing wake dataset. Rows correspond to the streamwise velocity, crosswise velocity, and pressure fields. The probe locations are indicated by cross symbols in the upper-left subplot.
Figure 15: Temporal evolution of reconstruction errors for the velocity field (left) and pressure field (right) for the wing wake dataset.

Figure 16: Probability density functions of fluctuating velocity and pressure for the original PIV fields, LOR, and reconstructed fields for the wing wake dataset. All PDFs are computed over the testing set and normalized by the RMS of the fluctuations, u′ PIV and p′ PIV .
Figure 17: Temporal-frequency (top) and wavenumber (bottom) power spectra of the two velocity components for the PIV fields, LOR, and reconstructed fields for the wing wake dataset.
